summary: Gruber analyzes Fox's completely redesigned scorebug debuted during Super Bowl 59, arguing that despite near-universal negative reactions, the new design is objectively superior. He breaks down specific design choices — huge team initialisms with color-coded boxes replacing logos, elimination of decorative chrome, and bold typography — explaining why each improves clarity. He draws a parallel to the original 1994 'Fox Box' innovation, which was also widely panned but became the universal standard for sports broadcasting. The piece uses this as a launching point to examine humanity's innate resistance to change, arguing that visceral negative reactions to visual changes are evolutionary instincts, not design critiques.
key_points: Fox's new scorebug uses oversized team initialisms with color-coded backgrounds instead of team logos, which Gruber argues is more legible at all screen sizes and distances
The elimination of decorative chrome and 3D styling echoes Jony Ive's iOS 7 redesign — conveying information through type and color alone rather than needless decoration
The original Fox Box in 1994 — simply showing the score on screen at all times — was widely condemned as stupid by critics and rival networks, yet became the universal standard within years

Human beings have an evolutionary instinct to be alarmed by visual change, which explains why app icon changes and scorebug redesigns reliably generate outsized negative reactions regardless of quality
The new design works better on both the biggest screens (bars, restaurants) and the smallest (phones) — a feat the chrome-heavy old design couldn't achieve
money_quotes: Human beings evolved to be alarmed by change. If everything looks the same as usual, everything is probably as safe as usual. If something looks jarringly different, it might be a sign that you're about to be killed.
No change an app developer can make will generate more commentary and emotional feedback from users than changing the app's icon.
"Looks like something out of Fortnite" isn't the dis the last commenter thinks it is. A lot of people play and love Fortnite.
This isn't minimalism, per se, but it's a strike against needless decoration — reminiscent to my mind of Jony Ive's radical redesign of iOS 7 a decade ago.
The old Fox scorebug looks hopelessly dated; old-fashioned without any nostalgic charm. If anything, the new typography-first design is the one that looks timeless.
The networks literally thought it would be better if many viewers didn't know the score.
Who seems stupid now? Imagine the outcry if Fox had broadcast yesterday's Super Bowl without any scorebug at all.
Different doesn't always mean better. But better necessarily implies different.
key_insight: Visceral negative reactions to design changes are evolutionary instincts masquerading as aesthetic judgments — the same resistance that condemned the original on-screen scorebug in 1994 is now condemning its bold redesign.
